text: Hello and welcome to This is My Architecture.
I'm Tony and I'm here in New Zealand with Evgeny from Orion Health.
Great to be here.
So Evgeny, tell me a bit about what Orion Health does.
So we're a healthcare IT organization who's got more than 55 deployments globally.
Traditionally, we'd built our software for clinicians,
but these days patients and consumers are getting more and
more engaged with their own care and we build solutions for that too on the population health scale.
And DFD is actually one of our new products.
It's digital front door.
Okay, so I understand the front door of a clinic and I understand how that's used to access healthcare.
But what is a digital front door?
So imagine patient who's looking for information online about their health.
For instance, they might have some medical condition or they're taking care of someone who's got medical condition.
They would try and search for trusted health information online.
They would want to book appointments with health service providers, find those service providers.
Essentially, they want 24-7 access to health.
And then on the other hand side, we've got clinicians who are looking into managing those patients
and they're interested in reducing the load on their systems.
So essentially, they want some sort of pre-filtering of those patients.
And what we want to do is to bring the right person at the right time, get them to the right care.
So with DFD website and Advisor Portal, we can do that.
So DFD website pulls some data from the CMS.
There are lots of things behind it as well to provide services for patients.
Advisor Portal actually pulls data from Health Information Platform.
And that's where clinicians are able to see patients' records.
And then patients can actually go and search for information they need in a single place at any time they want.
Okay. So obviously, search is important here.
And I see the Kendra icon in the middle here.
How does the intelligent search in Amazon Kendra help with the digital front door?
So that's one of the most interesting parts of DFD.
So when we go on digital front door website, we have the search bar.

And in that search bar, the user is able to perform search across multiple things like health service organizations, medical conditions, make bookings, and actually search for the health provider that fits them.
So in Kendra, we actually have the index that contains a lot of documents.
Essentially, there might be millions of documents and it scales quite well as well.
And that Kendra index actually provides APIs which we expose by API gateways to that search UI web component.
Cool. And you see millions of documents.
I imagine there's a huge amount of data in healthcare.
How do you actually index all of that data?
So what we do, we use built-in Kendra web crawler.
That is able to crawl static resources.
That could be static websites.
Essentially, the list of data sources that we validate with clinicians and provide to the index.
And then we also have the custom data crawler that we built using Lambda that is able to crawl dynamic resources.
Pull data out of them.
It also uses DynamoDB as a queue.
And it loads those documents that it pulls from dynamic resources into S3 bucket.
And then from S3 bucket, Kendra seamlessly pulls those documents into the index, processes them, and provides the search results to patients.
So one of the cool things about Kendra is also that it's natural language processing search.
So whenever you ask a question in the search bar, it will give you the exact answer rather than providing you with keyword based search result.
And that's how it works.
Cool. Thanks for sharing that with us.
It's fantastic to hear how the intelligent search in Amazon Kendra is able to help users get better access to their healthcare.
Thanks for helping us.
And thank you for watching This Is My Architecture.
